senses.definition: Recognizes or admits that something is true, real, or valid.
examples: He finally agnizes the role he played in the failure.
The court agnizes her right to appeal.
The community slowly agnizes the need for change.

senses.definition: Recognizing or admitting that something is true, real, or valid.
examples: Agnizing his mistake, he wrote a letter of apology.
The committee is agnizing the importance of transparency.

senses.definition: Members of an early Christian group who believed that Christ did not know some things, such as the time of the Last Judgment.
examples: The Agnoetae were criticized for teaching that Christ lacked full knowledge.
Historians debate how widespread the beliefs of the Agnoetae really were.

senses.definition: A member of the Agnoetae, an early Christian group who said that Christ was ignorant of some things.
examples: The bishop condemned him as an Agnoete.
As an Agnoete, he argued that Christ did not know the day of judgment.

senses.definition: The belief, held by the Agnoetae, that Christ did not know some things, especially the time of the Last Judgment.
examples: Agnoetism was rejected as heresy by the wider church.
The council met to discuss the spread of agnoetism.

senses.definition: Systems or studies of ignorance and how it is produced or maintained.
examples: Different agnoiologies can explain why some facts remain hidden from the public.
Critical scholars sometimes propose new agnoiologies to analyze the politics of knowledge.

senses.definition: The philosophical study of ignorance, especially how and why it exists.
examples: Agnoiology asks what kinds of ignorance are produced in a society and for whose benefit.
Her book on agnoiology explores how institutions keep people uninformed.

senses.definition: A member of a Christian group that taught Christ did not fully know certain things, especially the time of the Last Judgment.
examples: The monk was denounced as an agnoite by his opponents.
Agnoite beliefs raised difficult questions about Christ’s nature.

senses.definition: Members of a Christian group that claimed Christ was ignorant of some things, such as the date of the Last Judgment.
examples: The agnoites were active in parts of the Eastern church.
Councils wrote against the errors of the agnoites.

senses.definition: Small Italian pasta squares filled with meat, cheese, or vegetables, usually served with sauce.
examples: We ordered pumpkin agnolotti with sage butter.
The restaurant’s signature dish is beef agnolotti in red wine sauce.

senses.definition: In ancient Rome, an extra name given to a person, often to mark an honour, achievement, or characteristic.
examples: The general earned the agnomen "Africanus" after his victory in Africa.
An agnomen could celebrate a military success or a personal trait.

senses.definition: Plural of agnomen: extra names given, especially in ancient Rome, to mark honours or special qualities.
examples: He lists the agnomens of several famous Roman leaders.
These agnomens record both victories and defeats.

senses.definition: Relating to an agnomen, especially an extra name marking honour or distinction.
examples: The historian discusses the agnomical traditions of Roman families.
These agnomical titles often referred to military achievements.

senses.definition: Plural of agnomen: extra names added to a person’s main name, especially in ancient Rome, to show honour or a special quality.
examples: The emperor’s full list of agnomina filled an entire line of text.
Many generals collected several agnomina during their careers.

senses.definition: Relating to an agnomen or to an extra name added to a person’s usual name.
examples: He analyzed the agnominal elements in the emperor’s full title.
Agnominal additions often celebrate military success.

senses.definition: A rhetorical play on words that sound similar, or the giving of a related or punning name.
examples: The poet uses agnomination to connect the hero’s name with his fate.
Agnomination can create memorable wordplay in political slogans.

senses.definition: A brain condition in which a person cannot recognize objects, people, sounds, or other things, even though their senses are working.
examples: After the stroke, she developed visual agnosia and could not recognize faces.
Agnosia can affect one sense, such as sight or hearing, while others remain normal.

senses.definition: Different types or cases of agnosia, in which people cannot recognize certain things even though their senses work.
examples: The textbook describes several agnosias that affect vision.
These agnosias are often linked to damage in specific brain areas.

senses.definition: Relating to or affected by agnosia, especially not being able to recognize things even when the senses work.
examples: The patient showed agnosic difficulties with everyday objects.
Her agnosic symptoms improved after therapy.

senses.definition: A person who has agnosia and cannot recognize certain things even though their senses are normal.
examples: An agnosic may see a key but be unable to name it.
The study followed several agnosics over ten years.

senses.definition: Loss or absence of the ability to recognize objects, people, or sounds, usually because of brain damage.
examples: Agnosis may follow a head injury or stroke.
The neurologist explained that agnosis was causing his confusion with everyday items.

senses.definition: A person who believes that it is impossible to know for sure whether God or any gods exist.
examples: She describes herself as an agnostic rather than an atheist.
Many agnostics prefer to suspend judgment about religious questions.

senses.definition: Not having a clear opinion about whether God exists, or believing that this cannot be known.
examples: He holds an agnostic position on the existence of God.
Her parents are agnostic but open to spiritual ideas.

senses.definition: Not strongly supporting any one side or choice, especially because the result does not matter to you.
examples: The company is platform-agnostic and works with many systems.
As a coach, she is agnostic about playing style as long as the team wins.

senses.definition: Relating to or showing the belief that it is impossible to know whether God exists.
examples: He writes from an agnostical standpoint.
Her agnostical view keeps her open to different religious ideas.

senses.definition: In a way that shows you do not claim to know whether God exists, or that you stay neutral about a choice.
examples: She speaks agnostically about questions of faith.
The software is designed to work agnostically across different operating systems.

senses.definition: The belief that people cannot know for sure whether God or any gods exist, or that they lack enough evidence to decide.
examples: Agnosticism became more common as scientific thinking grew.
His agnosticism is based on a demand for strong evidence.

senses.definition: A general attitude of not taking a firm position when something cannot be known or measured.
examples: Scientific agnosticism warns against making claims beyond the data.
Her political agnosticism makes her wary of all parties.

senses.definition: Different forms or expressions of agnosticism as a belief or attitude.
examples: Modern thinkers describe several agnosticisms rather than a single position.
These agnosticisms vary in how open they are to religious belief.

senses.definition: People who believe that it is impossible to know for sure whether God or any gods exist.
examples: The group includes believers, atheists, and agnostics.
Many agnostics prefer honest uncertainty to forced belief.

senses.definition: A fossil trilobite of the genus Agnostus, found in some very old rock layers.
examples: Agnostus fossils help geologists date Cambrian rocks.
The museum’s display includes several specimens of Agnostus.

senses.definition: Lack of knowledge or understanding, especially in a philosophical or theological context.
examples: He admits a certain agnosy about the ultimate nature of reality.
Their shared agnosy leads them to avoid firm claims about God.

senses.definition: Relating to animals or life forms that are not yet known or have not been described by science.
examples: The deep ocean may still hide agnotozoic species.
Agnotozoic fauna challenge our ideas of what life can be.

senses.definition: A lamb, especially in religious art or language referring to Christ as the Lamb of God.
examples: The painting shows an agnus carrying a cross.
The choir sang the Agnus Dei at the end of the mass.

senses.definition: Plural of agnus: lambs, especially used as religious symbols.
examples: The church windows are filled with agnuses and other sacred images.
Medieval carvings of agnuses often carry banners or crosses.

senses.definition: Used with a period of time to say how far back in the past something happened.
examples: I moved here two years ago.
She called me a few minutes ago.
Long ago, people traveled by horse.

senses.definition: Very excited and eager to see or hear something.
examples: The children were agog with excitement before the show.
Fans were agog to learn more about the new game.
Social media was agog with rumors after the announcement.

senses.definition: The strict public education and training system used for boys in ancient Sparta.
examples: The agoge aimed to produce brave and disciplined soldiers.
Boys entered the agoge at a young age and lived under harsh rules.

senses.definition: Plural of agoge: systems of strict public education and training, especially in ancient Sparta.
examples: Different Greek states had their own agoges.
The agoges shaped not only soldiers but also citizens.

senses.definition: Relating to slight changes in the length of musical notes to make the rhythm more expressive.
examples: The pianist added agogic accents to highlight the melody.
Agogic variation can make a simple rhythm sound more alive.

senses.definition: The study or use of small rhythmic changes in music to make it more expressive.
examples: Her research focuses on agogics in late Romantic piano music.
Good agogics can bring out the emotional shape of a phrase.

senses.definition: A tall evergreen tree, similar to a pine, found in coastal areas of Southeast Asia and the Pacific.
examples: The shore was lined with agoho trees.
Local builders sometimes use agoho wood for light structures.

senses.definition: In motion or working; usually used in older or dialect speech.
examples: Once the engine is agoing, it runs smoothly.
They could hear the mill wheels agoing all night.

senses.definition: A chemical substance (peptide) produced in the body that can affect blood pressure and blood vessels.
examples: Researchers measured levels of agomensin in the patients’ blood.
Agomensin may play a role in controlling blood pressure.

senses.definition: A condition in which teeth are missing.
examples: Severe agomphiasis can affect chewing and speech.
The dentist noted agomphiasis in the lower jaw.

senses.definition: Having no teeth or missing some teeth.
examples: An agomphious jaw can make eating difficult.
The agomphious patient required special dental care.

senses.definition: A situation in which a tooth is not firmly fixed in its socket, or the absence of the normal joint between tooth and bone.
examples: Trauma can lead to agomphosis of the front teeth.
Agomphosis may require surgical treatment.

senses.definition: In ancient Greek drama, a formal debate or contest between two characters.
examples: The central agon of the play sets the hero against the chorus leader.
In comedy, the agon often opposes old and new values.

senses.definition: A struggle or intense conflict, especially in a literary or psychological sense.
examples: The novel explores the inner agon of a man facing death.
Her poems capture the agon of artistic creation.

senses.definition: Relating to the struggle just before death, especially irregular breathing or movement.
examples: The patient showed agonal breathing before the heart stopped.
Agonal movements can occur in the final moments of life.

senses.definition: A long time ago; used in older or literary English.
examples: Many years agone, this valley was filled with farms.
The tale was first told ages agone.

senses.definition: Past or gone by; used in older English after a word for time.
examples: Six months agone, the town was quiet.
The events of winters agone still shaped their fears.

senses.definition: Plural of agon: formal contests or struggles, especially in ancient Greek drama or athletics.
examples: The play’s agones oppose different political ideals.
Religious festivals in Greece included athletic agones.

senses.definition: A state of intense suffering, often close to death; used mainly in older or literary English.
examples: He lay in agonia for several hours.
The poem describes the agonia of a wounded soldier.

senses.definition: A poetic or literary work that focuses on struggle, suffering, or heroic pain.
examples: Her latest book is an agoniada of a nation at war.
The film plays like an agoniada of personal loss.

senses.definition: A bitter chemical substance obtained from certain plants, sometimes used in medicine or research.
examples: Agoniadin was isolated from the seeds for laboratory testing.
Scientists are studying how agoniadin affects the nervous system.
